name: Volume

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - 17-question real past-paper set on Volume
2025_q1 page 79 Q140 (sphere melted into a wire, with answer key — found while researching an earlier topic, stashed and used here)

researchNotes: This is the last of Part B Unit I's 10 named Aptitude skills. Strong web-sourced evidence (17 real past-paper questions found on one aggregator page) plus the previously-stashed sphere-melted-into-wire problem. Picked a representative spread across the recurring shapes: ratio-of-volumes-from-ratio-of-radii, melting/recasting (volume conservation), and surface-area-to-volume relationships — skipped near-duplicate ratio questions (multiple sourced items tested the exact same 'ratio of radii → ratio of volumes' pattern with different numbers). Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only — also used Day 9, already completed): added cone volume, hemisphere volume, the cuboid-scaling principle (same cube-of-scale-factor rule as spheres), water-displacement/rise problems, and melting-into-multiple-smaller-spheres. Every question, new and pre-existing, now carries the mandatory Part-B `trick`. All arithmetic re-verified by hand, favoring clean numbers where possible.

reading.intro: Volume is the last of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows two dominant shapes: 'ratio of dimensions → ratio of volumes' (scaling), and 'melt/recast one solid into another shape' (volume is conserved, surface area is not) — both are core techniques worth knowing cold.

qa.question: Method — ratio of volumes from a ratio of radii (same shape): If the radius of one sphere is half that of another, what is the ratio of their volumes?
qa.answer: Volume scales with the CUBE of linear dimensions (V=4/3πr³), not directly with the radius. So if r₁:r₂=1:2, then V₁:V₂ = 1³:2³ = 1:8.

qa.question: Method — melting and recasting (volume is conserved, NOT surface area): A sphere of radius 9cm is melted and recast into a cone of the same radius (9cm). Find the cone's height.
qa.answer: Set sphere volume = cone volume: (4/3)π(9)³ = (1/3)π(9)²h → (4/3)(729) = (1/3)(81)h → 972 = 27h → h = 36 cm. The general shortcut: for melting sphere→cone of the SAME radius, height = 4×radius always (since the (4/3)r³ vs (1/3)r²h relationship reduces to h=4r when radii match).

qa.question: Real exam (already seen in an earlier topic, re-noted here): A sphere of diameter 8cm is melted and drawn into a wire of diameter 3mm. Find the wire's length.
qa.answer: Volume conserved: (4/3)π(4)³ = π(0.15)²×h → h ≈ 3792.6 cm ≈ 37.9 m. (Same melt/recast principle as r02, just sphere→cylinder/wire instead of sphere→cone.)

qa.question: Method — volume of a cylindrical tank from its capacity: A cylindrical tank has capacity 1,848 m³ and base diameter 14m. Find its depth.

Method — dug-out soil redistributed into a different shape (volume conserved): A cylindrical well of depth 20m, diameter 14m is dug; the soil is spread evenly to form a cuboid platform of base 20m×14m. Find the platform's height.
qa.answer: Well volume = (22/7)×7²×20 = 3,080 m³. Platform volume = 20×14×h = 280h. Set equal: 280h=3080 → h=11 m.

qa.question: Formula check: volume of a cube with side 5cm, and the relationship between a cube's volume and surface area.
qa.answer: Volume = side³ = 5³ = 125 cm³. If a cube's volume is 1000 cm³, its side = ∛1000 = 10cm, so its surface area = 6×side² = 6×100 = 600 cm² (cube surface area = 6×side², from its 6 square faces).

qa.question: Method — ratio of volumes for cone, sphere, cylinder of equal radius AND equal height: What is that ratio?
qa.answer: Cone : Sphere : Cylinder = 1 : 4 : 3, when all three share the same radius r and the same height h (for the sphere, 'height' = its diameter = 2r in this comparison). This is a frequently tested ratio worth memorizing directly.

qa.question: What is the formula for the volume of a cone, and what is it for radius 6cm, height 7cm?
qa.answer: V = (1/3)πr²h. For r=6, h=7: (1/3)×(22/7)×36×7 = (1/3)×22×36 = (1/3)×792 = 264 cm³ (the 7 in the height cancels neatly with the 7 in π=22/7).

qa.question: What is the formula for the volume of a hemisphere, and what is it for radius 21cm?
qa.answer: V = (2/3)πr³. For r=21: (2/3)×(22/7)×9261. Since 9261÷21=441, this simplifies to (2×22×441) = 19,404 cm³ — choosing a radius that's a multiple of 7 keeps the arithmetic clean.

qa.question: If all three dimensions of a cuboid are doubled, by what factor does its volume increase?
qa.answer: By a factor of 2×2×2=8 — the same 'cube of the scale factor' principle that applies to spheres and any other 3D shape when ALL linear dimensions scale uniformly.

qa.question: Method — rise in water level from a submerged solid: A solid metal cube of side 6cm is dropped into a rectangular tank of base 20cm×15cm containing water. Find the rise in water level.
qa.answer: The cube displaces its own volume of water: 6³=216 cm³. Rise in level = displaced volume ÷ tank base area = 216/(20×15) = 216/300 = 0.72 cm.
qa.tip: Displacement problems: rise in level = volume of the submerged object ÷ the container's (unchanged) base area — you never need the container's height.

qa.question: Method — pouring liquid between differently-sized cylindrical vessels: A cylindrical vessel of radius 6cm and height 15cm is full of water; the water is poured into a wider cylindrical vessel of radius 9cm. Find the height risen in the second vessel.
qa.answer: Volume is conserved: π×6²×15 = π×9²×h → 540 = 81h → h = 540/81 ≈ 6.67 cm (the π cancels from both sides, so it never needs to be evaluated).

qa.question: Method — melting one sphere into many smaller identical spheres: A sphere of radius 6cm is melted into small spheres of radius 1.5cm each. How many small spheres can be made?
qa.answer: Since volume is conserved and volume scales with the cube of radius: number of small spheres = (R/r)³ = (6/1.5)³ = 4³ = 64.

questions.prompt: If the radius of sphere A is 3 times the radius of sphere B, what is the ratio of their volumes (A:B)?
questions.trick: Volume ratio = (linear dimension ratio)³ — cube the given ratio directly.

questions.prompt: A solid sphere of radius 6cm is melted and recast into a cone of the same radius. Find the cone's height.
questions.explanation: Using the shortcut h=4×radius for sphere→same-radius-cone: h=4×6=24cm.
questions.trick: Sphere melted into a same-radius cone: height = 4×radius, always — a direct shortcut from equating the two volume formulas.

questions.prompt: A cylindrical tank has capacity 4,620 m³ and base diameter 14m. Find its depth.
questions.trick: V=πr²h rearranged for h=V/(πr²) — always find r from the diameter first.

questions.prompt: Find the volume of a cube with side 8cm.
questions.trick: Volume of a cube = side³ — the most basic 3D formula.

questions.prompt: If a cube's volume is 2,197 cm³, find its surface area.
questions.trick: Always recover the side length (cube root of volume) FIRST, then apply 6×side² for surface area — never confuse the two formulas directly.

questions.prompt: A cone, a sphere, and a cylinder share the same radius and same height. What is the ratio of their volumes (cone:sphere:cylinder)?
questions.explanation: Cone:Sphere:Cylinder = 1:4:3 for equal radius and height.
questions.trick: Memorize 1:4:3 directly — re-deriving it from three separate formulas under time pressure wastes valuable minutes.

questions.prompt: Find the volume of a hemisphere with radius 21cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Volume of hemisphere = (2/3)πr³ — exactly half a full sphere's (4/3)πr³.

questions.prompt: If all three dimensions of a cuboid are doubled, by what factor does its volume increase?
questions.trick: Uniform linear scaling by factor k always multiplies volume by k³, for ANY 3D shape — not just spheres.

questions.prompt: A solid metal cube of side 6cm is dropped into a rectangular tank of base 20cm×15cm containing water. Find the rise in water level.
questions.trick: Rise in level = volume of submerged object ÷ container's base area — the container's height is irrelevant.

questions.prompt: Find the volume of a cone with radius 3.5cm and height 12cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: V=(1/3)πr²h — plug directly, and look for cancellations with π's denominator when the radius is a multiple/fraction of 7.

questions.prompt: A cylindrical vessel of radius 6cm and height 15cm, full of water, is poured into a wider cylindrical vessel of radius 9cm. Find the height risen in the second vessel.
questions.trick: Volume is conserved when transferring liquid — set the two πr²h expressions equal and the π's cancel out immediately.

questions.prompt: A sphere of radius 6cm is melted into small spheres of radius 1.5cm each. How many small spheres can be made?
questions.trick: Number of smaller identical pieces = (original radius ÷ smaller radius)³ — cube the ratio, don't just take it directly.

questions.prompt: Find the volume of a cylinder with radius 7cm and height 10cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: V=πr²h — a radius that's a multiple of 7 always cancels cleanly with π=22/7.

questions.prompt: Find the volume of a rectangular cuboid with dimensions 12cm×8cm×5cm.
questions.trick: Volume of a cuboid = length×breadth×height — the most basic 3D formula, multiply all three dimensions directly.
